column1: # Source: Z-Beam Laser Cleaning Research Lab
# Citation: Z-Beam (2025). Beryllium Laser Cleaning Dataset. Retrieved from https://www.z-beam.com/materials/metal/specialty/beryllium-laser-cleaning

Optimal average power range for Beryllium oxide layer removal
Near-IR wavelength optimized for Beryllium thermal absorption characteristics
Beam spot diameter for precise cleaning control
High repetition rate for efficient cleaning with minimal thermal damage
Fluence threshold for Beryllium oxide removal without substrate damage

Typical range for 1064 nm Nd:YAG lasers in metal cleaning to balance efficiency and heat input; starting at 150 W for Beryllium due to high reflectivity (~70% at 1064 nm) reducing absorption; max avoids substrate melting (1287°C) per studies on reflective metals (e.g., SPIE Proc. Vol. 10912, 2019). Source: IPG Photonics YLR series specs.

Range for effective contaminant removal without excessive heat accumulation; 1000 mm/s recommended for optimal balance on Beryllium's high thermal conductivity (216 W/mK); slower (200-500 mm/s) for heavy contamination, faster (up to 3000 mm/s) for light. Source: Laserax equipment manuals and J. Laser Appl. 32, 022064 (2020).

Nanosecond regime (5-100 ns) typical for industrial cleaning to ablate contaminants while minimizing heat-affected zone (HAZ); 20 ns recommended for Beryllium to limit thermal diffusion given its conductivity; picosecond (ps) or femtosecond (fs) for ultra-precise but costlier applications. Source: Laser physics review in Opt. Lasers Eng. 120, 102-115 (2019).

10-100 kHz for cleaning applications; 50 kHz recommended for Beryllium to achieve fast processing without significant heat buildup (high freq increases speed but risks accumulation on reflective surfaces); trade-off noted in ablation efficiency. Source: Academic study in Appl. Surf. Sci. 475, 245-252 (2019).

Fluence range for contaminant removal on metals; Beryllium ablation threshold ~2-3 J/cm² at 1064 nm, so 1.5 J/cm² recommended for safe cleaning below damage; higher for heavy contamination but conservative due to reflectivity. Source: Ablation threshold studies in J. Phys. D: Appl. Phys. 52, 395301 (2019).

100-500 μm beam diameter for cleaning; 200 μm recommended for Beryllium in industrial surface prep to ensure uniform energy delivery without pinpoint damage; smaller for fine details, larger for broad areas. Source: Coherent Inc. laser cleaning system specifications.

1-5 passes typical for complete removal; 2 passes recommended for Beryllium to achieve thorough cleaning of contaminants while avoiding cumulative thermal stress; single pass for light layers, multi for heavy. Source: Industry case studies in Proc. SPIE 11080, 110800M (2019).

30-80% overlap for uniform coverage in scanning; 50% recommended for Beryllium to ensure even fluence distribution and prevent gaps, optimizing for its reflective properties. Source: Process optimization in Surf. Coat. Technol. 378, 124-130 (2019).
